claim: Microsoft called to say my computer has a virus and they need remote access to fix it
explanation: Microsoft never makes unsolicited phone calls about computer problems. This is a classic tech support scam where criminals pretend to be from Microsoft, Apple, or your ISP to trick you into giving them remote access to your computer, then steal your data or charge you for fake repairs.

claim: A pop-up says my computer is infected and I need to call this number immediately
explanation: Legitimate virus alerts never tell you to call a phone number. These pop-ups are fake warnings designed to scare you into calling scammers who will try to get remote access to your computer or charge you for unnecessary software.

claim: Your internet service will be disconnected unless you pay immediately with gift cards
explanation: No legitimate company asks for payment in gift cards. Your ISP will send written notices before disconnecting service and would never demand immediate gift card payment. This is a common tech support scam variation.

claim: Apple Support says my iCloud account has been compromised and they need my password to secure it
explanation: Apple will never call you unsolicited and ask for your password. No legitimate tech company asks for your password over the phone. If you're concerned about your account, go directly to apple.com or call Apple's official number yourself.
red_flags: Unsolicited call claiming to be Apple

claim: A technician says they detected suspicious activity on my router and needs to install security software
explanation: No one is monitoring your home router for suspicious activity and calling you about it. This is a tech support scam. The 'security software' they want to install is actually malware or remote access tools that let them control your computer.

claim: Windows Defender has expired and I need to call this number to renew my subscription
explanation: Windows Defender (now called Microsoft Defender) is free and built into Windows — it doesn't have a subscription that expires. Any pop-up or email telling you to call a number to renew it is a scam.

claim: Geek Squad sent an invoice for $399 auto-renewal that I need to call to cancel
explanation: Fake Geek Squad renewal invoices are one of the most common email scams. If you didn't sign up for a Geek Squad plan, ignore the email. The phone number leads to scammers who will try to get remote access to your computer or your bank information.
red_flags: Auto-renewal you didn't sign up for

claim: Amazon detected unauthorized purchases on my account and needs me to verify my payment info
explanation: Amazon won't call you about unauthorized purchases and ask for your payment details. If you're worried, log into your Amazon account directly (type amazon.com in your browser — don't click links in emails) and check your order history.
red_flags: Unsolicited call claiming to be Amazon

claim: I met someone online who works on an oil rig and needs money for an emergency
explanation: The 'oil rig worker' is one of the most common romance scam personas. Scammers create fake profiles, build emotional connections over weeks or months, then fabricate emergencies to ask for money. Real romantic partners you've never met in person won't ask you for money.
red_flags: Claims to work on oil rig or overseas

claim: My online boyfriend who is a military officer overseas needs money for leave paperwork to come visit me
explanation: Military leave never requires payment. Scammers often pose as deployed military members because it explains why they can't meet in person or video chat. The U.S. military does not charge soldiers for leave, and soldiers have free access to communication tools.
red_flags: Claims to be military deployed overseas
Request for money for military paperwork

claim: Someone I met on a dating site needs money to pay customs fees to ship a package to me
explanation: Customs fees scams are a classic romance scam tactic. The 'package' doesn't exist, and the customs fees are fake. Legitimate customs fees, if they existed, would be paid by the shipper or collected by a recognized shipping company, not wired to an individual.

claim: I met someone wonderful online who wants me to invest in cryptocurrency with them
explanation: This is a 'pig butchering' scam — romance scammers build trust, then steer victims toward fake cryptocurrency investments. They may show you a fake trading platform with impressive 'returns.' The platform is fake, and any money you send goes straight to the scammers.
red_flags: Online romance leading to investment advice
Fake trading platform with guaranteed returns

claim: My online partner is stuck in another country and needs money for a plane ticket to come see me
explanation: Sending money for travel expenses to someone you've only met online is a huge red flag. Romance scammers always have reasons they can't meet — and always need money to overcome the obstacle. The trip never happens, and they'll have a new excuse and a new request.

claim: My online love interest needs money for hospital bills after a sudden accident
explanation: Medical emergency money requests are a top romance scam tactic. Scammers fabricate emergencies to create urgency and emotional pressure. Real people in real emergencies have insurance, family, or local resources — they don't ask online acquaintances for money.

claim: A widow or widower I met online has an inheritance but needs help paying legal fees to access it
explanation: The 'inheritance that needs fees' story combines romance scam and advance-fee fraud tactics. There is no inheritance. Legitimate inheritances are handled by lawyers and courts — no one needs a stranger's help paying legal fees to access their own money.
red_flags: Request to help with legal costs

claim: The IRS is calling to say I owe back taxes and a warrant has been issued for my arrest
explanation: The IRS never threatens arrest over the phone. They never demand immediate payment or call to collect without first sending a written notice by mail. If you owe taxes, the IRS sends letters — they don't threaten you with arrest on a phone call.
red_flags: Claim of arrest warrant for taxes
Phone call claiming to be IRS

claim: Social Security Administration called to say my Social Security number has been suspended due to suspicious activity
explanation: Your Social Security number cannot be 'suspended.' The SSA will never call to threaten you, ask for payment, or request your SSN over the phone. This is one of the most common government impersonation scams targeting older adults.

claim: Medicare is calling to send me a new card but they need to verify my Medicare number first
explanation: Medicare will never call you unsolicited to verify your Medicare number. Scammers use this tactic to steal your Medicare ID, which they use for identity theft and fraudulent billing. If you need a new card, call 1-800-MEDICARE directly.

claim: A government agent says I need to pay a fine immediately using gift cards or I'll be arrested
explanation: No government agency accepts gift cards as payment. Period. The government sends fines through official mail and accepts payment through standard methods. Anyone demanding gift cards is a scammer — 100% of the time.

claim: The county sheriff's office called about a missed jury duty and I need to pay a fine over the phone
explanation: Courts don't call about missed jury duty demanding phone payment. If you actually missed jury duty, you'd receive an official letter. Scammers use the threat of legal trouble for missing jury duty because it scares people into paying immediately.
red_flags: Phone call about missed jury duty

claim: The DEA says my name was found on a package of illegal drugs and I need to verify my identity to clear my name
explanation: The DEA and other law enforcement agencies do not call citizens to tell them their name was found on drug packages. This is a government impersonation scam designed to scare you into sharing personal information or sending money.
red_flags: Request to verify identity over phone

claim: A utility company says my power will be shut off in one hour unless I make an immediate payment
explanation: Utility companies are required to give advance written notice before shutting off service — they don't make threatening phone calls with one-hour deadlines. If you're unsure, hang up and call the number on your utility bill directly.

claim: Grandma it's me I'm in jail and I need bail money right away please don't tell mom and dad
explanation: This is the classic 'grandparent scam.' Scammers call pretending to be a grandchild in trouble, often with a fake lawyer on the line. They count on your love and worry to make you act fast. Always verify by calling your grandchild directly on their known phone number.
red_flags: Instruction not to tell other family

claim: Your grandson was in a car accident in another state and needs money wired for medical bills
explanation: Scammers use car accident stories to create panic. They may even know your grandchild's name from social media. Before sending any money, hang up and call your grandchild directly. If they don't answer, call their parents. Real emergencies can wait 5 minutes for verification.

claim: A lawyer called saying my grandchild was arrested abroad and needs money for legal fees and to keep it confidential
explanation: The 'arrested abroad' variant adds a fake lawyer to make the scam seem more legitimate. Real lawyers don't cold-call grandparents demanding wire transfers. And a real legal situation would involve the embassy, not a random phone call asking for secrecy.

claim: Your nephew needs money sent via Zelle right now because he's stranded and his phone is broken
explanation: Scammers create scenarios where the family member can't be reached to verify the story — broken phone, lost wallet, etc. If someone says they're a family member in trouble, hang up and call that person directly. If you can't reach them, call another family member.
red_flags: Urgency to send money via payment app

claim: I got a call saying my grandchild needs money for bail and I should send it via Bitcoin ATM
explanation: No bail system accepts Bitcoin. This combines the grandparent scam with cryptocurrency to make the money untraceable. Courts accept cash bail, bail bonds, or sometimes credit cards — never cryptocurrency from a Bitcoin ATM.

claim: AI-generated voice of my grandchild called asking for emergency money
explanation: Scammers now use AI to clone voices from social media videos. Even if it sounds exactly like your grandchild, it could be fake. Always hang up and call your grandchild back on their real number. Set up a family code word that only your family knows for emergencies.

claim: Congratulations you have won the Spanish National Lottery and need to pay a processing fee to claim your prize
explanation: You can't win a lottery you didn't enter. There is no 'Spanish National Lottery' that contacts foreign winners. All lottery scams follow the same pattern: you 'won' something, but need to pay fees before you can collect. Real lotteries deduct fees from winnings.
red_flags: Processing fee required to claim prize

claim: You've been selected to receive a $500,000 grant from the government but need to pay the tax upfront
explanation: The government doesn't randomly select people for grants, and legitimate grants never require upfront payment. If you have to pay money to receive money, it's a scam. Government grants require applications and are awarded for specific purposes.

claim: Publishers Clearing House says I won $1 million and they need my bank info to deposit the prize
explanation: Publishers Clearing House (PCH) delivers major prizes in person with their famous Prize Patrol — they don't call or email asking for bank information. Scammers impersonate PCH because it's a trusted name. PCH will never ask you to pay fees or share bank details to claim a prize.
red_flags: Prize notification via phone or email

claim: I received a check for $5,000 from a sweepstakes and need to wire back $500 for taxes before I can cash it
explanation: This is a fake check scam. The check looks real and may even clear temporarily, but it's fraudulent. When the bank discovers it's fake (which can take weeks), you'll owe the full amount back — plus the $500 you wired to the scammers is gone forever.

claim: You've won a free cruise vacation but need to pay port fees and taxes to claim your trip
explanation: Free cruise offers that require upfront fees are always scams. Legitimate companies don't charge fees to claim prizes. The 'cruise' either doesn't exist or comes with so many hidden costs and restrictions that it's worthless.

claim: You won a car in a raffle and need to pay $2,000 in delivery and registration fees
explanation: Winning a car you never entered a raffle for is a clear scam signal. Even in legitimate raffles, delivery and registration fees are never thousands of dollars paid upfront to the organizer. This is an advance-fee scam using a car as bait.

claim: Your bank account has been compromised please click this link to verify your identity and secure your account
explanation: Banks don't send emails or texts with links asking you to verify your identity. This is a phishing scam — the link leads to a fake website that looks like your bank but steals your login credentials. Always go directly to your bank's website by typing the URL yourself.

claim: Your Netflix subscription payment failed click here to update your billing information
explanation: Netflix and other streaming services are commonly impersonated in phishing emails. If your payment actually failed, Netflix will show a notice when you log in. Don't click email links — open Netflix directly in your browser to check your account status.

claim: USPS says your package cannot be delivered and you need to update your address through this link
explanation: Fake package delivery texts and emails are extremely common phishing scams. USPS, UPS, and FedEx don't text you links to 'update your address.' If you're expecting a package, track it directly on the carrier's official website.

claim: Your email account will be deactivated in 24 hours unless you confirm your password
explanation: Email providers don't threaten to deactivate your account and ask for your password via email. This is a phishing scam designed to steal your email credentials, which gives scammers access to all your other accounts through password resets.

claim: PayPal detected unusual activity on your account click here to confirm your identity
explanation: PayPal phishing emails are very common and can look extremely convincing. PayPal will never ask you to click an email link to confirm your identity. If you're concerned, open a new browser tab, go to paypal.com directly, and log in to check your account.

claim: Your Amazon order has shipped click here to track it
explanation: If you didn't order anything from Amazon, this is a phishing email. Even if you did order something, don't click links in emails — go to amazon.com directly and check 'Your Orders.' Phishing links lead to fake sites designed to steal your login credentials.

claim: Your bank needs you to verify recent transactions by entering your debit card PIN
explanation: Your bank will never ask for your PIN via email, text, or phone. Your PIN is something only you should know. Banks have their own fraud detection systems and will freeze suspicious transactions — they don't need you to enter your PIN to verify them.

claim: Invest in this new cryptocurrency and get guaranteed 500% returns in 30 days
explanation: No legitimate investment guarantees returns — especially not 500%. Guaranteed returns are the #1 sign of investment fraud. All real investments carry risk, and anyone who says otherwise is either lying or running a Ponzi scheme.

claim: This exclusive investment opportunity is only available for the next 48 hours and spots are limited
explanation: Legitimate investments don't use high-pressure sales tactics with artificial deadlines. Scammers create urgency to prevent you from doing research or consulting a financial advisor. Any real investment opportunity will still be there after you take time to think about it.

claim: Put your retirement savings into this risk-free gold and silver investment for maximum returns
explanation: There's no such thing as a 'risk-free' investment with 'maximum returns.' Precious metals investments have real risks, and scammers specifically target retirees because they have savings. Always consult a licensed, independent financial advisor before making major investment decisions.

claim: Join our forex trading program and earn $10,000 per week from home with no experience needed
explanation: Forex (foreign exchange) trading is legitimate but extremely complex and risky. Promises of easy, guaranteed income with no experience are classic investment scam hallmarks. Professional traders lose money regularly — the idea that beginners earn $10K/week is absurd.

claim: This new AI trading bot has a 99% win rate and will double your money every month
explanation: No trading bot has a 99% win rate. If it did, the creator would be the richest person alive and wouldn't be selling it to you. AI trading scams use buzzwords like 'artificial intelligence' and 'machine learning' to sound sophisticated, but they're just stealing your money.

claim: Please donate to help hurricane victims through this GoFundMe link we just set up
explanation: After natural disasters, fake charity scams explode. Scammers set up fake GoFundMe pages, create fake charity websites, and make emotional appeals. Always donate through established charities like the Red Cross, and verify any charity at give.org or charitynavigator.org.

claim: Support our veterans fund by donating over the phone with your credit card right now
explanation: Fake veteran charities are one of the most common charity scams. Scammers use emotional appeals about veterans to pressure phone donations. Legitimate charities don't pressure you for immediate credit card donations over the phone. Check charitynavigator.org before donating.
red_flags: Pressure for immediate credit card payment

claim: A charity is going door-to-door collecting cash donations for children with cancer
explanation: While some legitimate charities do door-to-door campaigns, collecting cash at the door is a red flag. Legitimate charities provide written materials, are registered, and typically accept checks or online donations. Ask for their charity registration number and verify at give.org.

claim: Our church is collecting donations via wire transfer for a mission trip to help orphans overseas
explanation: Wire transfer requests are a major red flag even from seemingly legitimate organizations. Scammers sometimes impersonate churches or religious organizations. Verify directly with your church leadership in person, and donate through the church's established channels, not wire transfers.
